facts.desc: chromosomes that are identical in shape, size, and gene loci, one inherited from each parent
facts.function: Pair up during Prophase I to allow for crossing over and genetic variation
facts.caps_misconceptions: Learners often confuse homologous chromosomes with sister chromatids. Homologous chromosomes have the same genes but may have different alleles, while sister chromatids are identical copies.
facts.examiner_notes: Always emphasize that they come in maternal and paternal pairs.

facts.desc: cells containing only one set of chromosomes (n)
facts.importance: Ensures the chromosome number does not double during fertilization, maintaining species stability.
facts.caps_misconceptions: Learners sometimes think haploid means having half of a chromosome (a single chromatid). It actually means having half the number of chromosomes.

facts.desc: cells containing two sets of chromosomes (2n)
facts.importance: Contains the full genetic blueprint, combining traits from both parents.

facts.desc: the structure that holds two sister chromatids together in a replicated chromosome
facts.function: Serves as the attachment point for spindle fibers during cell division.
facts.examiner_notes: Identify the centromere in a diagram and state its function. Distinguish between centromere and centriole.

facts.desc: protein structures that contract to pull chromosomes or chromatids to the poles during cell division
the thread-like network of DNA and proteins found in the nucleus during interphase
two identical copies of a chromosome produced by DNA replication and joined by a centromere
an organelle near the nucleus of a cell that contains the centrioles (in animal cells) and from which the spindle fibers develop
The point of contact where crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids
facts.function: Physical link where genetic material is exchanged

facts.desc: A protein structure on the centromere where spindle fibers attach during cell division
facts.function: Attachment site for spindle fibers to pull chromatids apart

facts.desc: Chromatids belonging to homologous chromosomes that undergo crossing over
facts.function: Exchange genetic material during crossing over to increase genetic variation

facts.desc: The physical process of cell division, which divides the cytoplasm of a parental cell into two daughter cells
A condition where a cell has an abnormal number of chromosomes, such as 45 or 47 in humans
A genetic disorder caused by the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 21 (Trisomy 21)
A photographic arrangement of a complete set of chromosomes of a cell, arranged in homologous pairs according to size
facts.function: Used to determine the sex of an individual or detect chromosomal abnormalities

facts.desc: The first 22 pairs of chromosomes in a human cell that determine general body traits
The 23rd pair of chromosomes in humans that determine the sex of the individual (XX or XY)
The pairing of two homologous chromosomes that occurs during meiosis
Chromatids that contain a new combination of alleles as a result of crossing over
The process of egg (ovum) formation in females through meiosis
facts.results_in: One mature ovum and three polar bodies

facts.desc: The process of sperm formation in males through meiosis
A small cell produced during oogenesis that contains little cytoplasm and typically degenerates
Protein structures that pull apart genetic material in a cell during cell division
facts.function: Attach to kinetochores and pull chromosomes/chromatids to opposite poles

facts.desc: A cylindrical organelle composed mainly of a protein called tubulin
The division of the cell nucleus during cell division
The central plane of the cell where chromosomes align during metaphase
The opposite ends of a cell to which chromosomes or chromatids are pulled during anaphase
A genetic disorder in which a person has three copies of a chromosome instead of two
A genetic condition in which a person has only one copy of a chromosome instead of two
A chromosomal disorder in which a female is born with only one X chromosome
A chromosomal disorder in which a male is born with an extra X chromosome (XXY)
A change in the DNA sequence that can lead to variation or genetic disorders
A mutation that occurs in a somatic cell and is not passed on to offspring
A mutation that occurs in a gamete (sperm or egg) and can be passed on to offspring
A mature haploid male or female germ cell that is able to unite with another of the opposite sex in sexual reproduction to form a zygote
A diploid cell resulting from the fusion of two haploid gametes
The process of combining the male gamete, or sperm, with the female gamete, or ovum
Differences in physical traits of an individual from the group to which it belongs
Variation in a phenotypic trait in which there is a complete range of values
Variation in a phenotypic trait in which traits are grouped into discrete categories
The chromosome in a homologous pair that is inherited from the mother
The chromosome in a homologous pair that is inherited from the father
DNA found outside the nucleus, such as in mitochondria or chloroplasts
DNA found within mitochondria, inherited exclusively from the mother
DNA found within chloroplasts in plant cells
Proteins around which DNA is wound to form chromatin
facts.function: Package and order DNA into structural units called nucleosomes

facts.desc: A structural unit of a eukaryotic chromosome, consisting of a length of DNA coiled around a core of histones
The specific physical location of a gene or other DNA sequence on a chromosome
the phase of meiosis where homologous chromosomes pair up and crossing over occurs
facts.importance: Introduces genetic variation through crossing over.
facts.examiner_notes: Be able to draw or identify this phase showing crossing over.

facts.desc: the phase where homologous chromosome pairs align randomly at the equator of the cell
events: Bivalents line up on the equator
Spindle fibers attach to the centromeres

facts.desc: the phase where entire chromosomes (consisting of two chromatids) are pulled to opposite poles
events: Homologous chromosomes are pulled to opposite poles
facts.results_in: Halving the chromosome number (diploid to haploid)
facts.examiner_notes: Note that centromeres do NOT split in Anaphase I, unlike in Anaphase II or Mitosis.
facts.caps_misconceptions: Learners often say chromatids separate here. Chromosomes (each with two chromatids) separate.

facts.desc: the phase resulting in the formation of two haploid cells, each containing double-stranded chromosomes
the phase where the spindle fibres form in the two newly formed haploid cells without further DNA replication
the phase where individual chromosomes align singly at the equator of the cell
the phase where the centromere splits and sister chromatids separate to move to opposite poles
the final phase of meiosis where four genetically unique haploid cells are formed
a pair of homologous chromosomes closely associated during Prophase I
the resting phase before meiosis begins, during which DNA replication occurs
the exchange of genetic material between non-sister chromatids of a bivalent, promoting genetic variation
facts.results_in: Recombinant chromatids with new combinations of alleles
facts.caps_misconceptions: Learners often say crossing over happens between sister chromatids. It MUST be non-sister chromatids.

facts.desc: the random arrangement of maternal and paternal homologous chromosomes at the equator during Metaphase I
facts.results_in: Different combinations of maternal and paternal chromosomes in the gametes

facts.desc: the specific points of attachment where crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids
the failure of homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids to separate correctly during meiosis
facts.results_in: Aneuploidy (gametes with an extra or missing chromosome)

facts.desc: a genetic disorder (Trisomy 21) caused by an extra copy of chromosome 21 due to non-disjunction
haploid sex cells produced as a result of meiosis, essential for sexual reproduction
another term for meiosis I, as it halves the chromosome number from diploid to haploid
the diversity in gene combinations among offspring, largely resulting from crossing over and independent assortment
a reproductive strategy where eggs are laid outside the body and embryos develop externally
facts.requires: Yolk as a food source for the developing embryo
facts.advantages: Less energy required from the mother after laying, large numbers of eggs can be produced
facts.disadvantages: Eggs are vulnerable to predators and environmental changes

facts.desc: a reproductive strategy where embryos develop inside eggs that are retained within the mother's body until hatching
facts.requires: Yolk for nutrition (no placental connection to mother)
facts.advantages: Protection from predators and environmental extremes while developing
facts.disadvantages: Fewer offspring produced compared to ovipary

facts.desc: a reproductive strategy where embryos develop inside the mother's body and obtain nourishment directly via a placenta
facts.requires: Placenta (in most cases) for nutrient and gas exchange
facts.advantages: High survival rate of offspring due to maximum protection and continuous nutrient supply
facts.disadvantages: High energy cost to the mother, fewer offspring produced

facts.desc: a developmental strategy where offspring are born or hatched fully developed, able to move and feed independently
a developmental strategy where offspring are born or hatched naked, blind, and highly dependent on parental care
The period of development of the embryo and fetus inside the mother's body
The process of keeping eggs warm outside the body to allow the embryo to develop
A profound change in form from one stage to the next in the life history of an organism
An organ that develops in the uterus during pregnancy, providing oxygen and nutrients to the growing baby and removing waste products from the baby's blood
facts.function: Exchange of materials between mother and fetus

facts.desc: A flexible cord-like structure containing blood vessels and attaching a human or other mammalian fetus to the placenta during gestation
A mammal of an order whose members are born incompletely developed and are typically carried and suckled in a pouch on the mother's belly
A primitive mammal that lays large yolky eggs and has a common opening for the urogenital and digestive systems
A mammal characterized by the presence of a placenta, which facilitates exchange of nutrients and wastes between the blood of the mother and that of the fetus
the union of sperm and egg outside the body of the female, typical in aquatic environments
facts.requires: Water or a moist environment to prevent gametes from drying out and allow sperm to swim
facts.disadvantages: Low chance of fertilization, gametes are vulnerable to predators and environment
facts.advantages: Produces a large number of offspring without high energy cost to the mother

facts.desc: the union of sperm and egg inside the body of the female, maximizing reproductive success in terrestrial environments
facts.advantages: Higher chance of fertilization, gametes are protected from harsh environments
facts.disadvantages: Requires more complex reproductive systems and energy

facts.desc: the physical act of mating allowing for the direct transfer of sperm into the female for internal fertilization
the simultaneous release of large numbers of eggs and sperm into the water, characteristic of external fertilization
an egg with a shell and extra-embryonic membranes adapted for survival in terrestrial environments
the fluid-filled sac that surrounds and cushions the developing embryo against mechanical shock
the outermost extra-embryonic membrane primarily involved in gaseous exchange
the extra-embryonic membrane sac that collects metabolic wastes and assists with gaseous exchange
the extra-embryonic membrane that stores and provides nourishment to the developing embryo
the liquid within the amnion that protects the embryo from mechanical shock and temperature changes
the investment of time and energy by parents to increase the survival chances of their offspring
the egg white in an amniotic egg that provides water and additional protein to the embryo
male organs located in the scrotum responsible for the production of sperm and testosterone
facts.function: Produce sperm (spermatogenesis) and secrete testosterone
facts.location: Suspended outside the abdominal cavity in the scrotum

facts.desc: the highly coiled tube on top of the testis where sperm mature and are temporarily stored
facts.function: Temporary storage of sperm while they mature and gain motility
facts.location: Attached to the posterior surface of the testis

facts.desc: the muscular tube that transports sperm from the epididymis to the urethra
facts.function: Propels sperm during ejaculation via peristalsis

facts.desc: glands that secrete a nutrient-rich fluid containing fructose to provide energy for sperm
a gland that secretes an alkaline fluid to neutralize the acidic environment of the vagina and urethra
facts.function: Secretes alkaline fluid to neutralize the acidic environment of the vagina, protecting sperm
facts.location: Base of the bladder, surrounding the urethra

facts.desc: a small gland that secretes a lubricating fluid into the urethra before ejaculation
the highly coiled microscopic tubes within the testes where spermatogenesis takes place
supporting cells in the seminiferous tubules that provide nourishment to developing sperm
cells located between the seminiferous tubules that secrete the hormone testosterone
the cap-like structure at the head of a sperm cell containing enzymes to penetrate the ovum
Cells located in the interstitial tissue between seminiferous tubules that secrete testosterone
facts.function: Produce and secrete the hormone testosterone
facts.location: Between the seminiferous tubules in the testes

facts.desc: A mixture of sperm and the glandular secretions from the seminal vesicles, prostate, and Cowper's glands
facts.function: Provides a liquid medium for sperm to swim and provides nutrients and protection

facts.desc: A pituitary hormone that stimulates the development of a Graafian follicle in the ovary
facts.function: Stimulates the maturation of a primary follicle into a Graafian follicle

facts.desc: A solid ball of cells formed by the rapid mitotic divisions of the zygote
A hollow ball of cells consisting of an inner cell mass and an outer trophoblast layer
facts.function: The stage of the embryo that implants into the endometrium
facts.location: Reaches the uterus roughly 5-7 days after fertilization

facts.desc: The fluid within the amniotic sac that surrounds and protects the developing fetus
function: Allows free movement for fetal development

facts.desc: female gonads responsible for the production of ova, oestrogen, and progesterone
facts.function: Produce ova via oogenesis and secrete estrogen and progesterone
facts.location: Pelvic cavity, one on each side of the uterus

facts.desc: the muscular tubes lined with cilia where fertilization typically occurs in humans
facts.function: Site of fertilization; transports the fertilized egg to the uterus
facts.location: Between the ovaries and the uterus
facts.examiner_notes: Always identify the Fallopian tube as the site of fertilization.

facts.desc: the thick-walled, muscular organ where the embryo implants and develops during pregnancy
facts.function: Houses and nourishes the developing fetus

facts.desc: the inner lining of the uterus that is richly supplied with blood vessels and thickens during the menstrual cycle
the lower, narrow neck of the uterus that opens into the vagina
facts.function: Produces mucus to regulate sperm entry; dilates during childbirth
facts.location: Between the uterus and the vagina

facts.desc: the mature fluid-filled ovarian follicle that ruptures to release a secondary oocyte during ovulation
the glandular structure formed from the ruptured follicle after ovulation, which secretes progesterone
facts.function: Secretes large amounts of progesterone to maintain the thickened endometrium
facts.examiner_notes: If fertilization does NOT occur, it degenerates. If it DOES occur, it persists to maintain pregnancy until the placenta takes over.

facts.desc: the outer layer of cells covering the ovary, from which primary follicles develop
the release of a mature ovum from the Graafian follicle into the fallopian tube, usually around day 14
Follicle Stimulating Hormone, secreted by the pituitary, which stimulates the development of primary follicles in the ovary
Luteinizing Hormone, secreted by the pituitary, which triggers ovulation and the formation of the corpus luteum
the hormone produced by the growing follicle that initiates the thickening of the endometrium and inhibits FSH
the hormone produced by the corpus luteum that maintains the thick, highly vascular endometrium for pregnancy
function: Inhibits FSH release (prevents further follicle development)
facts.decreases_when: The corpus luteum degenerates (leading to menstruation)

facts.desc: the shedding of the thickened endometrial lining accompanied by bleeding when fertilization does not occur
facts.caused_by: A sharp drop in progesterone and estrogen levels

facts.desc: the first half of the ovarian cycle during which a follicle matures under the influence of FSH
the second half of the ovarian cycle characterized by the presence and function of the corpus luteum
the temporary organ formed from maternal and embryonic tissue that facilitates the exchange of nutrients, gases, and wastes
the hollow tube connecting the fetus to the placenta, typically containing two umbilical arteries and one umbilical vein
the attachment and embedding of the blastocyst into the thickened endometrial lining of the uterus
the period of development of the embryo and fetus in the uterus from fertilization to birth
finger-like projections from the chorion that embed into the endometrium to form the embryonic part of the placenta
the hormone secreted by the pituitary gland that stimulates strong uterine contractions during labour
the pituitary hormone that stimulates the mammary glands to produce milk after birth
the primary command center of the nervous system consisting of the brain and spinal cord
all the cranial and spinal nerves extending from the brain and spinal cord to the rest of the body
a specialized neuron (afferent) that carries nerve impulses from receptors to the central nervous system
a specialized neuron (efferent) that carries nerve impulses from the central nervous system to effectors (muscles or glands)
a multipolar neuron entirely within the CNS that connects sensory and motor neurons
the microscopic gap between the axon terminal of one neuron and the dendrite of the next, where neurotransmitters diffuse
a chemical substance released at a synapse that transmits an impulse across the synaptic gap
a fatty insulating layer around many axons that speeds up the transmission of nerve impulses
The part of the nervous system consisting of the brain and spinal cord
facts.function: Processes, interprets, and stores information; issues orders to muscles and glands

facts.desc: The part of the nervous system consisting of all the nerves outside the CNS
facts.function: Transmits information to and from the CNS

facts.desc: The part of the PNS that controls involuntary actions such as heartbeat and digestion
facts.function: Maintains internal homeostasis without conscious thought

facts.desc: The division of the autonomic nervous system that prepares the body for 'fight or flight' in emergencies
facts.function: Increases heart rate, dilates pupils, inhibits digestion

facts.desc: The division of the autonomic nervous system that controls 'rest and digest' functions
facts.function: Slows heart rate, stimulates digestion, returns body to normal after a stress response

facts.desc: The structural and functional unit of the nervous system; a specialized nerve cell
facts.function: Transmits electrical impulses

facts.desc: A neuron that carries impulses from a receptor to the central nervous system
facts.function: Transmits sensory information to the brain or spinal cord
facts.structure: Usually unipolar or bipolar, cell body located in the dorsal root ganglion

facts.desc: A neuron that carries impulses from the central nervous system to an effector (muscle or gland)
facts.function: Initiates a response in an effector
facts.structure: Multipolar, cell body located in the gray matter of the spinal cord

facts.desc: Sensory receptors located in the utricle and saccule of the inner ear
facts.function: Detect the position of the head relative to gravity (static balance)

facts.desc: the largest part of the brain responsible for voluntary actions, conscious thought, memory, and processing sensory information
function: Responsible for higher thought processes, memory, and judgment

facts.desc: the part of the brain located behind the brainstem, responsible for coordinating voluntary muscle movements and maintaining balance
facts.function: Coordinates voluntary muscle movements and maintains balance and posture
facts.examiner_notes: If a person is drunk and stumbling, the cerebellum is the part of the brain primarily affected.

facts.desc: the lowest part of the brainstem that controls involuntary vital functions such as breathing rate and heartbeat
facts.function: Controls involuntary, life-sustaining functions such as heartbeat, breathing rate, and blood pressure
facts.location: Connects the brain to the spinal cord

facts.desc: the brain region that maintains homeostasis (e.g., body temperature, thirst) and links the nervous and endocrine systems
function: Links the nervous system to the endocrine system via the pituitary gland

facts.desc: a broad band of nerve fibers joining the two hemispheres of the cerebrum, allowing them to communicate
facts.function: Allows communication between the two cerebral hemispheres

facts.desc: the three protective connective tissue membranes that surround the brain and spinal cord
facts.function: Provide physical protection and contain cerebrospinal fluid

facts.desc: a rapid, automatic, and involuntary response to a stimulus designed to protect the body from harm
the specific structural neural pathway followed by an impulse during a reflex action
a specialized sensory cell or structure that detects a specific stimulus and converts it into a nerve impulse
a muscle or gland that brings about a specific response to a stimulus after receiving an impulse from a motor neuron
the transparent, curved front part of the sclera that allows light to enter the eye and causes the most refraction
facts.function: Allows light to enter the eye and causes the greatest refraction (bending) of incoming light
facts.characteristics: Avascular (no blood vessels) to remain transparent

facts.desc: the pigmented, muscular ring that controls the size of the pupil and the amount of light entering the eye
facts.function: Controls the amount of light entering the eye by adjusting the size of the pupil (pupillary mechanism)

facts.desc: the transparent, biconvex, elastic structure that changes shape to focus light precisely onto the retina
facts.function: Changes shape to focus light rays accurately onto the retina (accommodation)

facts.desc: the inner, light-sensitive layer of the eye containing photoreceptors (rods and cones)
facts.function: Converts light stimuli into nerve impulses

facts.desc: the reflex process by which the ciliary muscles alter the curvature of the lens to focus on objects at varying distances
the reflex action of the iris muscles that alters pupil size in response to varying light intensities
the structure containing muscles that control the tension on the suspensory ligaments to change the shape of the lens
tough, inelastic fibers that hold the lens in position and attach it to the ciliary body
the central area of the retina containing a high concentration of cones, responsible for the sharpest vision
the point on the retina where the optic nerve exits the eye, lacking photoreceptors
photoreceptors in the retina sensitive to low light intensities, providing black-and-white vision
photoreceptors in the retina sensitive to bright light and responsible for color vision and visual acuity
the visible, outer cartilaginous part of the ear that acts as a funnel to collect and direct sound waves
facts.function: Collects and directs sound waves into the auditory canal

facts.desc: the thin, tightly stretched membrane (eardrum) separating the outer and middle ear which vibrates in response to sound waves
facts.function: Vibrates when struck by sound waves and transmits these vibrations to the ossicles

facts.desc: the three small interconnected bones (malleus, incus, stapes) in the middle ear that transmit and heavily amplify vibrations
facts.function: Transmit and amplify vibrations from the tympanic membrane to the oval window

facts.desc: the tube connecting the middle ear to the pharynx, functioning to equalize air pressure on both sides of the tympanic membrane
facts.function: Equalizes air pressure on both sides of the tympanic membrane to prevent it from bursting

facts.desc: the coiled, fluid-filled bony structure in the inner ear containing the Organ of Corti for hearing
facts.function: Converts mechanical vibrations into nerve impulses (hearing)

facts.desc: the specialized receptor structure within the cochlea that converts mechanical vibrations into nerve impulses
three fluid-filled, loop-like structures in the inner ear responsible for dynamic balance and detecting changes in speed or direction
the membrane-covered opening from the middle ear to the inner ear, against which the stapes vibrates to create pressure waves in the cochlear fluid
the membrane at the base of the cochlea that bulges outward to absorb pressure waves and prevent echo in the inner ear
a highly vascularized, ductless gland that secretes hormones directly into the bloodstream
a gland that secretes its products into ducts that lead to external surfaces or specific cavities (e.g., salivary glands)
the small 'master gland' located at the base of the brain that secretes many hormones controlling other endocrine glands
facts.function: Secretes hormones that control the activity of other endocrine glands

facts.desc: the butterfly-shaped gland located in the neck that secretes thyroxin to regulate metabolism
facts.function: Regulates the basal metabolic rate of the body

facts.desc: the paired glands situated immediately above the kidneys that secrete adrenaline and aldosterone
facts.function: Prepares the body for emergencies and regulates salt and water balance

facts.desc: a dual-function organ acting as both an exocrine gland (digestive juice) and an endocrine gland (insulin and glucagon)
the specific clusters of endocrine cells within the pancreas that secrete insulin (beta cells) and glucagon (alpha cells)
The specific organ or tissue upon which a hormone acts
facts.characteristics: Contains specific receptors on its cell membranes that recognize and bind to the specific hormone

facts.desc: The process of maintaining a constant internal environment within the body despite external changes
facts.examples: Regulation of body temperature, blood sugar, water balance, and CO2 levels

facts.desc: a chemical messenger, usually a protein or steroid, produced by an endocrine gland and transported in blood to target organs
the iodine-containing hormone that regulates the basal metabolic rate, growth, and development of the body
the 'fight or flight' hormone that rapidly prepares the body for physical emergencies by increasing heart rate and blood glucose
the hormone secreted by beta cells of the pancreas that lowers blood glucose levels by promoting its conversion to glycogen in the liver
facts.function: Stimulates cells to absorb glucose from the blood and converts excess glucose into glycogen for storage

facts.desc: the hormone secreted by alpha cells of the pancreas that raises blood glucose levels by stimulating the breakdown of glycogen
facts.function: Stimulates the breakdown of stored glycogen back into glucose, releasing it into the blood

facts.desc: the pituitary hormone that specifically stimulates the thyroid gland to secrete thyroxin
the steroid hormone from the adrenal cortex that regulates sodium ion reabsorption in the kidneys
facts.target_organ: Kidneys (distal convoluted tubule and collecting duct)
facts.function: Increases the reabsorption of sodium ions from the kidney tubules back into the blood

facts.desc: the pituitary hormone that increases the permeability of kidney collecting ducts to water, promoting water reabsorption
a control system where an increase or decrease in a substance triggers a response that reverses the change, restoring a set point
a chronic metabolic disease resulting from inadequate insulin production or insulin resistance, leading to abnormally high blood glucose
the physical enlargement of the thyroid gland, often due to an iodine deficiency attempting to compensate for low thyroxin levels
the continuous physiological process of maintaining a relatively constant internal environment despite external changes
the specific homeostatic process of maintaining a constant internal body temperature (around 37°C in humans)
the widening of dermal blood vessels to increase blood flow to the skin, maximizing heat loss through radiation
the narrowing of dermal blood vessels to restrict blood flow to the skin, minimizing heat loss in cold conditions
the active release of a watery fluid onto the skin surface which draws large amounts of heat from the body as it evaporates
rapid, involuntary, and rhythmic muscle contractions that generate significant metabolic heat to warm the body
Sensory receptors that detect changes in the chemical composition of the blood, such as CO2 levels
facts.function: Detects high levels of CO2 (which lowers blood pH) and sends impulses to the medulla

facts.desc: The part of the brainstem that controls the rate and depth of breathing
facts.response_to_CO2: Sends impulses to the heart and breathing muscles to increase heart rate and breathing rate
facts.result: Excess CO2 is exhaled faster, returning blood levels to normal

facts.desc: the homeostatic regulation of water and dissolved solute (salt) concentrations in the blood and tissue fluids
the hormone that acts on the distal convoluted tubules and collecting ducts of nephrons to increase their permeability to water
the adrenal hormone that stimulates the active reabsorption of sodium ions from the kidney tubules into the blood
the brain region containing chemoreceptors that monitor carbon dioxide concentration and pH levels in the blood
the frequency of ventilation which is reflexively increased by the medulla oblongata to expel excess carbon dioxide
the frequency of cardiac contractions which increases to transport CO2-rich blood more rapidly to the lungs
plant hormones (e.g., IAA) produced at the tip of stems and roots, primarily responsible for cell elongation and tropisms
plant hormones that promote dramatic stem elongation, seed germination by breaking dormancy, and flowering
the inhibitory plant hormone that maintains seed dormancy and forces stomatal closure during times of drought stress
the phenomenon where high concentrations of auxins produced at the apical bud strongly inhibit the growth of lateral buds
synthetic auxins selectively used in high concentrations to cause rapid, unsustainable growth leading to the death of broad-leaved weeds
A plant hormone that promotes cell elongation, apical dominance, and tropisms
produced_in: Apical meristems of shoots and roots
facts.destroyed_by: Light (or it migrates away from light)

facts.desc: A plant hormone that stimulates stem elongation, seed germination, and flowering
facts.examiner_notes: Distinguish from auxin: Gibberellins mainly cause elongation of the internodes (the stem between leaves).

facts.desc: An inhibitory plant hormone that induces dormancy and helps the plant cope with stress
function: Causes stomata to close during drought conditions

facts.desc: Light that shines on a plant from one direction only
facts.causes: An unequal distribution of auxins in the stem tip

facts.desc: a slow, permanent growth movement of a plant organ in response to a unidirectional external stimulus
the directional growth movement of a plant stem or root in response to a unilateral light stimulus
the directional growth movement of a plant stem or root in response to the force of gravity
an experimental apparatus consisting of a slow-rotating disc used to completely eliminate the effect of a unidirectional stimulus like gravity
the growth of plant stems or shoots directly toward a unilateral light source, driven by auxin migration to the shaded side
the growth of plant roots downward in the direction of gravity, as high auxin concentration inhibits root cell elongation
the growth of plant stems upward away from the direction of gravity
the evolutionary strategy of producing toxic, irritating, or unpalatable secondary metabolites to deter herbivores from feeding
the presence of physical structural adaptations like thorns, spines, prickles, or thick cuticles to physically protect against herbivores
the natural phenomenon where atmospheric gases trap low-energy infrared radiation (heat), keeping the Earth sufficiently warm for life
the unnatural, enhanced greenhouse effect resulting in a steady increase in the Earth's average atmospheric and ocean temperatures
a measure of the total amount of greenhouse gases, specifically carbon dioxide, produced directly and indirectly by human activities
the dangerous thinning of the protective ozone layer in the stratosphere, primarily caused by chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)
a potent greenhouse gas released primarily from landfill sites, agricultural livestock (cattle), and coal mining
Non-native plant species that spread rapidly and outcompete indigenous plants
facts.impact_on_water: They consume vast amounts of water, reducing water availability in catchments

facts.desc: The variety of plant and animal life in a particular habitat or in the world
the severe ecological degradation of water bodies caused by excessive nutrient runoff (nitrates and phosphates) from fertilizers
a rapid, explosive overgrowth of algae on the surface of a water system that blocks sunlight from reaching submerged plants
a measurement of the massive amount of dissolved oxygen required by aerobic decomposing bacteria to break down organic matter in polluted water
the degradation of water quality caused by releasing heated water from industrial power plants back into natural ecosystems
non-indigenous invasive species that consume massive volumes of ground water and outcompete local flora for resources
the desirable state where all people, at all times, have physical, social, and economic access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food
the damaging agricultural practice of growing a single crop species repeatedly over a massive area, which depletes soil nutrients and reduces biodiversity
toxic chemical substances used indiscriminately to kill pests, which often bioaccumulate and biomagnify within aquatic and terrestrial food chains
organisms whose genetic material has been deliberately altered using biotechnology to increase crop yield and resistance to pests
the large-scale, permanent clearing of indigenous forests for agriculture or urbanization, destroying critical habitats and massive carbon sinks
aggressive, fast-breeding non-native species that completely lack natural predators and rapidly outcompete indigenous species for vital resources
the illegal hunting, capturing, or harvesting of wild plants and animals, driving many species like rhinos and cycads toward extinction
the irreversible process by which once-fertile land becomes a barren desert, heavily accelerated by severe drought, overgrazing, and poor agricultural practices
a heavily engineered designated area where solid municipal waste is dumped, compacted, and covered daily with soil to minimize disease vectors
the industrial process of systematically collecting and processing used waste materials to manufacture entirely new materials and objects
the basic monomer building block of nucleic acids, consisting of a central 5-carbon sugar, a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
the specific 5-carbon sugar tightly bonded to a phosphate and a base in every DNA nucleotide
a purine nitrogenous base that exclusively forms two hydrogen bonds with Thymine in DNA, or Uracil in RNA
a purine nitrogenous base that always forms three strong hydrogen bonds exclusively with Cytosine
a pyrimidine nitrogenous base strictly found only in DNA molecules that pairs exclusively with Adenine
the weak chemical bonds securely holding complementary nitrogenous base pairs together across the center of a DNA molecule
the iconic twisted, anti-parallel, ladder-like structural shape characteristic of all DNA molecules
the forensic process of analyzing highly variable non-coding regions of DNA to conclusively identify individuals or determine biological relationships
specialized spherical proteins around which long DNA strands tightly coil to form organized chromatin network
A double-stranded, helical nucleic acid molecule that carries the genetic information of an organism
facts.function: Stores and transmits genetic information to control protein synthesis

facts.desc: A molecule found in DNA and RNA that encodes the genetic information
A pyrimidine nitrogenous base that pairs with Guanine in both DNA and RNA
The weak bond that holds the complementary nitrogenous bases together across the DNA double helix
facts.function: Allows the two DNA strands to separate easily during replication and transcription

facts.desc: The process by which DNA makes an exact copy of itself
facts.importance: Ensures that each daughter cell receives a complete set of genetic information during cell division

facts.desc: The enzyme responsible for adding free nucleotides to the exposed DNA template strands during replication
facts.function: Builds the new complementary DNA strands

facts.desc: A single-stranded nucleic acid involved in protein synthesis
A type of RNA that carries the genetic code from the DNA in the nucleus to the ribosomes
facts.location: Formed in nucleus, functions in cytoplasm

facts.desc: A type of RNA that carries specific amino acids to the ribosomes during translation
The process by which cells build proteins based on the genetic code in DNA
facts.importance: Proteins determine the structure and function of all cells (enzymes, hormones, structural components)

facts.desc: the vital process occurring during interphase where a DNA molecule makes an exact identical copy of itself before cell division
the original separated DNA strand that serves as a direct pattern for the attachment of free complementary nucleotides
the specific 5-carbon sugar present in all RNA nucleotides
the unique nitrogenous base found only in RNA that physically replaces Thymine and pairs with Adenine
the long single-stranded RNA molecule transcribed in the nucleus that carries the genetic code blueprint directly to the ribosome
the small clover-shaped RNA molecule that carries specific corresponding amino acids from the cytoplasm to the ribosome during translation
the highly regulated first stage of protein synthesis where a specific mRNA molecule is formed from a DNA template inside the nucleus
the second stage of protein synthesis where ribosomes physically read mRNA sequences to assemble amino acids into a specific polypeptide chain
a precise sequence of three consecutive nitrogenous bases on an mRNA strand that explicitly codes for one specific amino acid
a sequence of three exposed bases on a tRNA molecule that is perfectly complementary to a specific mRNA codon
the basic monomer or building block of all proteins, which are linked together in a highly specific sequence
the strong chemical bond formed directly between two adjacent amino acids during the translation phase of protein synthesis
the microscopic organelle located in the cytoplasm acting as the physical site where translation and protein assembly occurs
a visual, highly structured representation of the complete set of chromosomes in a cell, systematically arranged in homologous pairs by size and shape
the first 22 pairs of chromosomes in humans that are strictly identical in both males and females and do not determine sex
the critical 23rd pair of chromosomes that explicitly determine the biological sex of an individual (XX for female, XY for male)
the exact specific physical location or position of a particular gene on a chromosome
alternative, slightly different structural forms of the same specific gene, located at the exact same locus on homologous chromosomes

subtopics.name: Meiosis as a Source of Variation

facts.desc: the crucial genetic exchange of DNA segments between non-sister chromatids of a homologous pair, guaranteeing unique new allele combinations in gametes
the completely random, unpredictable alignment and subsequent separation of maternal and paternal chromosomes at the equator during Metaphase I
the catastrophic failure during anaphase I or II where homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids fail to separate and migrate to opposite poles
the resulting condition of having an abnormal, non-exact multiple number of chromosomes, such as 47 or 45 instead of the normal 46
the specific chromosomal condition of having three distinct copies of chromosome 21, resulting in the developmental disorder Down syndrome
the complete, specific genetic composition or paired allele combination of an organism for a particular inherited trait
the outward physical appearance, measurable characteristic, or functional expression of an organism directly resulting from its genotype
a powerful allele that completely masks the expression of a recessive allele and is fully expressed in the heterozygous condition
a masked allele that is only physically expressed in the phenotype when the organism is strictly homozygous for that trait
a genetic state of having two strictly identical alleles for a particular gene (e.g., TT or tt), also known as pure-breeding
a genetic state of having two completely different alleles for a particular gene (e.g., Tt), often called a hybrid
a standard type of inheritance where the dominant allele completely and entirely masks the presence of the recessive allele
a specific pattern of inheritance where the heterozygous phenotype is an intermediate, third blended phenotype between the two homozygous extremes
a pattern of inheritance where both distinct alleles are equally, fully, and simultaneously expressed in the heterozygous phenotype without blending
a highly structured visual chart that meticulously traces the inheritance pattern of a specific genetic trait across several consecutive generations in a family
The study of heredity and the variation of inherited characteristics
A specific segment of DNA on a chromosome that codes for a particular protein or trait
An alternative form or version of a gene situated at the same locus on homologous chromosomes
examples: Tall (T) and dwarf (t) alleles for height

facts.desc: A sex-linked recessive genetic disorder where the blood fails to clot properly
A sex-linked recessive genetic disorder characterized by the inability to distinguish between certain colors (usually red and green)
A mutation that involves a change in a single nucleotide base pair in DNA
An organism whose genetic material has been altered using genetic engineering techniques
The process of gradual change in the characteristics of a population of organisms over many generations
facts.importance: Explains the diversity of life on Earth

facts.desc: The preserved remains, impressions, or traces of organisms that lived in the past
facts.importance: Provides direct physical evidence of extinct species and transitional forms

facts.desc: The study of the geographical distribution of plants and animals
facts.importance: Species living in similar environments in different parts of the world may have similar adaptations due to convergent evolution

facts.desc: Structures in different species that have a similar basic anatomical plan but may perform different functions
facts.examples: The pentadactyl limb in humans, whales, bats, and birds
facts.indicates: Divergent evolution from a common ancestor

facts.desc: Structures in different species that perform similar functions but have different anatomical origins
facts.examples: Wings of birds and wings of insects
facts.indicates: Convergent evolution due to similar environmental pressures, NOT a recent common ancestor

facts.desc: Structures that have lost their original function over time and are usually reduced in size
facts.indicates: Evolutionary relationships to ancestors that fully utilized those structures

facts.desc: The obsolete theory of evolution proposing that acquired characteristics can be inherited by offspring
laws: Law of the inheritance of acquired characteristics
facts.why_rejected: We now know that acquired phenotypic changes (e.g., stretching a neck, building muscle) do not alter the DNA in gametes, so they cannot be passed on.

facts.desc: The theory of evolution proposed by Charles Darwin, based on natural selection
A theory proposing that evolution consists of long periods of little change (stasis) interrupted by short bursts of rapid speciation
facts.explains: The sudden appearance of new species in the fossil record and the lack of transitional fossils

facts.desc: The mechanism by which individuals with favorable traits are more likely to survive and reproduce in a specific environment
facts.results_in: Adaptation to the environment over time

facts.desc: The deliberate breeding of plants and animals by humans for desired traits
facts.different_from: Natural selection (humans choose the traits, not the environment)

facts.desc: The evolutionary process by which new, distinct biological species arise
The formation of new species due to a population being geographically isolated by a physical barrier
facts.mechanism: The isolated populations undergo natural selection differently due to different environments, eventually becoming reproductively isolated.

facts.desc: Mechanisms that prevent two different species from mating and producing fertile offspring
The ability to walk upright on two legs
advantages: Provides a better view over tall grass for spotting predators
Reduces surface area exposed to direct sun
anatomical_evidence: Foramen magnum positioned centrally under the skull

facts.desc: The large opening at the base of the skull through which the spinal cord passes
facts.importance_in_evolution: A forward/central position indicates bipedalism, while a backward position indicates quadrupedalism.

facts.desc: The biological family that includes humans, extinct human-like species, and the great apes
An extinct genus of early hominins found in Africa that were bipedal but had small brains
An early hominin species known as 'handy man' because they were the first to make stone tools
An extinct hominin species that was the first to use fire and migrate out of Africa
The theory that modern humans (Homo sapiens) evolved in Africa and then migrated to other parts of the world, replacing other hominin species
evidence: Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) shows highest genetic diversity in Africa
Oldest Homo sapiens fossils found in Africa

facts.desc: a complex genetic cross between two individuals that simultaneously involves tracing the inheritance of two entirely different genetic traits
phenotypic traits heavily controlled by genes uniquely located on the sex chromosomes, primarily the large X chromosome
a severe sex-linked recessive genetic disorder characterized by the complete inability of blood to clot normally due to a missing clotting factor
a common sex-linked recessive visual defect strictly located on the X chromosome, leading to significant difficulty distinguishing certain colors
the rare genetic situation characterized by the presence of more than two possible alleles for a single gene locus within a population, such as the ABO blood groups
a sudden, small-scale change in the exact sequence of nitrogenous bases in a single specific gene on a DNA molecule
a massive, large-scale change in the physical structure or absolute number of whole chromosomes in a cell
the direct, highly deliberate technological modification of an organism's characteristics by manipulating, inserting, or deleting its genetic material
the broad industrial use of living organisms, their biological systems, or their components to produce highly useful commercial products or processes
the exact scientific process of producing genetically identical copies of a specific cell, tissue, or entire organism through asexual methods
unique, completely undifferentiated biological cells that retain the incredible potential to rapidly develop into various specialized cell types
the slow, continuous process of gradual genetic and physical change in the characteristics of a population over many successive generations
the strictly preserved ancient remains, hard impressions, or mineralized traces of long-dead organisms securely found in sedimentary rock
the dedicated scientific study of fossils to deeply understand past life forms and trace their evolutionary history and lineages
anatomical structures in completely different species that share a remarkably similar basic underlying plan (e.g., pentadactyl limb), strongly indicating common ancestry
distinct structures that perform exactly the same function in different species but fundamentally differ in their basic anatomical structure and evolutionary origin
the specific study of the past and present geographical distribution patterns of existing and extinct plant and animal species across the continents
The study of heredity and the variation of inherited characteristics
A specific segment of DNA on a chromosome that codes for a particular protein or trait
An alternative form or version of a gene situated at the same locus on homologous chromosomes
examples: Tall (T) and dwarf (t) alleles for height

facts.desc: An allele that is always expressed in the phenotype, masking the presence of a recessive allele
An allele that is only expressed in the phenotype when the organism is homozygous for that trait
The genetic makeup of an organism, represented by the alleles it possesses
The physical appearance or observable characteristics of an organism resulting from its genotype
Having two identical alleles for a particular trait
Having two different alleles for a particular trait
A genetic cross where the dominant allele completely masks the recessive allele in the heterozygous condition
facts.results_in: Heterozygote has the same phenotype as the homozygous dominant parent

facts.desc: A genetic cross where neither allele is completely dominant, resulting in an intermediate, blended phenotype in the heterozygote
facts.examples: Red flower x White flower = Pink flower

facts.desc: A genetic cross where both alleles are equally dominant and are fully expressed in the heterozygote without blending
examples: AB blood group (A and B antigens)

facts.desc: Traits controlled by genes located on the sex chromosomes (usually the X chromosome)
facts.examiner_notes: Males (XY) only need one recessive allele to express a sex-linked condition, while females (XX) need two. Therefore, these conditions are more common in males.

facts.desc: A sex-linked recessive genetic disorder where the blood fails to clot properly
A sex-linked recessive genetic disorder characterized by the inability to distinguish between certain colors (usually red and green)
When a single gene has more than two possible alleles in a population
facts.examples: ABO Blood groups in humans (alleles IA, IB, i)

facts.desc: A mutation that involves a change in a single nucleotide base pair in DNA
The artificial manipulation and alteration of an organism's genetic material to produce a desired trait
An organism whose genetic material has been altered using genetic engineering techniques
The process of creating a genetically identical copy of a cell, tissue, or organism
Undifferentiated cells that have the ability to divide and develop into many different specialized cell types
facts.function: Can be used therapeutically to replace damaged tissues

facts.desc: Structures that have lost their original function over time and are usually reduced in size
facts.indicates: Evolutionary relationships to ancestors that fully utilized those structures

facts.desc: Mechanisms that prevent two different species from mating and producing fertile offspring
The biological family that includes humans, extinct human-like species, and the great apes
An early hominin species known as 'handy man' because they were the first to make stone tools
An extinct hominin species that was the first to use fire and migrate out of Africa
The theory that modern humans (Homo sapiens) evolved in Africa and then migrated to other parts of the world, replacing other hominin species
evidence: Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) shows highest genetic diversity in Africa
Oldest Homo sapiens fossils found in Africa

facts.desc: the historically rejected theory of evolution strictly based on the inheritance of acquired characteristics and the flawed law of use and disuse during an organism's lifetime
the widely accepted core theory of evolution boldly proposing natural selection as the driving mechanism for descent with modification
the fundamental environmental mechanism of evolution where organisms best adapted to their specific environment survive, successfully reproduce, and generously pass on their highly favorable alleles
the highly deliberate, human-driven selective breeding of plants and animals over generations to produce very specific desired traits (e.g., dog breeds)
the complex evolutionary process by which completely new, reproductively isolated biological species arise from a pre-existing ancestral species
the specific formation of a new species when a single population becomes physically and geographically isolated by an impassable physical barrier
the evolutionary hypothesis aggressively asserting that evolution primarily occurs in rapid, sudden bursts separated by extremely long periods of static non-change
the exclusive evolutionary taxonomic group that specifically includes all modern humans and their completely extinct obligate bipedal ancestors
the unique, habitual ability to walk upright strictly on two lower limbs, marking a massive key milestone early in human evolution
the critically large opening at the base of the skull through which the spinal cord passes, whose forward position is a massive indicator of upright bipedal posture
the internal measurable volume of the protective braincase inside the skull, widely used as an excellent indicator of evolutionary brain size
the extensively supported theory powerfully proposing that modern Homo sapiens evolved entirely in Africa before migrating outward to populate all other continents
an entirely extinct, highly famous genus of early hominins found extensively in Africa, displaying a crucial mix of both primitive ape-like and advanced human-like traits
the official scientific binomial name specifically designating all modern humans alive today
